story.title: Dr. Chen Models the Future of Metals
story.text:
My name is Dr. Sarah Chen. I work at the National Science Lab. I am a research scientist who studies complex systems.

My computer model looks at Rare Earth Metals. These special metals are in your phone and computer. They are also essential for electric cars and wind turbines. We get these metals from the Earth. The Mining Rate shows how fast we dig them up. A faster rate increases our supply today. We also get metals from old products. The Recycling Rate shows how much we reuse. More recycling also increases our available supply.

When the supply is high, the market price is low. When the supply is low, the price gets very high. A high price creates a Substitution Incentive. This pushes companies to find new materials. But here is the challenge. A high mining rate now depletes our future resources. My model helps us see these connections. We can test different choices. This helps us plan for a sustainable future.
think_abouts: What are two ways we get rare earth metals?
What happens to the price of metals when the supply is low?
story.go_deeper: How could developing a new material to replace a rare earth metal change the whole system?

margin_defs.definition: Many connected parts that affect each other.
Uses up or reduces a supply of something.
Able to be continued for a long time.

check_prompts: What does Dr. Chen use to study the rare earth metals system?

vocabulary.definition: A group of 17 metals needed for modern technology like phones and electric cars.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Our phones use many different rare earth metals to work.

vocabulary.definition: The speed at which we take materials from the Earth, often measured in tons per year.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The high mining rate gives us more metals for now.

vocabulary.definition: The percentage of used materials that we collect and process to use again.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A good recycling rate helps us save our planet's resources.

vocabulary.definition: The motivation to find a new material when one gets too expensive or hard to find.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The high price created a substitution incentive for engineers to find a replacement.

discussion_cards.prompt: If you were a leader, would you focus more on increasing the mining rate or the recycling rate? Why?
discussion_cards.frame: I would focus on the ___ rate because ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: What is one product you use every day that might have rare earth metals inside? How would your life change if we ran out of them?
discussion_cards.frame: One product I use is my ___. My life would change because ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Chen's model shows that high prices lead companies to find new materials. Do you think this is a good thing or a bad thing? Explain.
discussion_cards.frame: I think this is a ___ thing because ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
